#4420 

We convert an `ArrayItem` like `x(loc, loc)`, where `loc` is an array, to:

```fortran
do __1_t = lbound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 1), ubound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 1)
    do __2_t = lbound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 2), ubound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 2)
        __libasr__created__var__0__array_item(__1_t, __2_t) = x(loc(__1_t), loc(__2_t))
    end do
end do
```

### TODO:

with F77 validation suite FCVS_95 (see #480 ) demonstrated that our support for `IMPLICIT` statements was incomplete, particularly in that we only allow one _implicit-spec_ per _implicit-stmt_, instead of an _implicit-spec-list_.  An example statement that is now properly accepted is:
```f90
implicit real(i), integer(b)
```

 This PR adds that support, with changes in the AST grammar, the parser, AST symbol table visitor, and AST->src conversion.  AST->ASR conversion already supported this structure.

The PR also updates the test reference AST files, and adds the new test `implicit13`.  After these changes, FCVS_95 tests that use _implicit-spec-list_ no longer fail (on those statements, at least).

This closes #5352 
This closes #3113  ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5536/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5536/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2509616418","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/pull/5536#issuecomment-2509616418","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5536","id":2509616418,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6VlbUi","user":{"login":"certik","id":20568,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIwNTY4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/20568?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik","html_url":"https://github.com/certik","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-01T07:39:58Z","updated_at":"2024-12-01T07:39:58Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Awesome, thanks for this fix @pjh40. #5513 

Earlier , we were not visiting arguments of Elemental Function . This PR tries to incorporate this thing in array_op.

MRE 1
```
program test 
  integer :: A(3) = [1,2,3]
  logical :: X(3)
  integer :: tol = 2
  X = abs(A) <= max(tol, tol * maxval(abs(A)))
  print *, X
end program
```

Output 
```
(lf) ansh_mehta@DESKTOP-VJGRLON:/mnt/d/lfortran2/lfortran$ ./src/bin/lfortran try.f90
T    T    T
```

MRE 2
```
program test 
  integer :: A(3) = [1,2,3]
  logical :: X(3)
  integer :: tol = 2
  X = abs(A) <= max(tol,maxval(abs(A)))
  print *, X
end program
```

Output 
```
(lf) lordansh@LordAnsh:~/dev/lfort2/lfortran$ ./src/bin/lfortran try.f90
T    T    T
```

MRE 3
```
program test 
integer :: A(3,2) = reshape([1,2,3,4,5,6], [3,2])
integer :: X(2)
X = abs(sum(A, dim=1))
print *, X
end program
```

Output 
```
(lf) lordansh@LordAnsh:~/dev/lfort2/lfortran$ ./src/bin/lfortran try.f90
6    15

Earlier , we were not visiting arguments of Elemental Function . This PR tries to incorporate this thing in array_op.

MRE 1
```
program test 
  integer :: A(3) = [1,2,3]
  logical :: X(3)
  integer :: tol = 2
  X = abs(A) <= max(tol, tol * maxval(abs(A)))
  print *, X
end program
```

Output 
```
(lf) ansh_mehta@DESKTOP-VJGRLON:/mnt/d/lfortran2/lfortran$ ./src/bin/lfortran try.f90
T    T    T
```

MRE 2
```
program test 
  integer :: A(3) = [1,2,3]
  logical :: X(3)
  integer :: tol = 2
  X = abs(A) <= max(tol,maxval(abs(A)))
  print *, X
end program
```

Output 
```
(lf) lordansh@LordAnsh:~/dev/lfort2/lfortran$ ./src/bin/lfortran try.f90
T    T    T
```

MRE 3
```
program test 
integer :: A(3,2) = reshape([1,2,3,4,5,6], [3,2])
integer :: X(2)
X = abs(sum(A, dim=1))
print *, X
end program
```

Output 
```
(lf) lordansh@LordAnsh:~/dev/lfort2/lfortran$ ./src/bin/lfortran try.f90
6    15

statements","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Working with F77 validation suite FCVS_95 (see #480 ) demonstrated that our support for `IMPLICIT` statements was incomplete, particularly in that we only allow one _implicit-spec_ per _implicit-stmt_, instead of an _implicit-spec-list_.  An example statement that is now properly accepted is:
```f90
implicit real(i), integer(b)
```

 This PR adds that support, with changes in the AST grammar, the parser, AST symbol table visitor, and AST->src conversion.  AST->ASR conversion already supported this structure.

The PR also updates the test reference AST files, and adds the new test `implicit13`.  After these changes, FCVS_95 tests that use _implicit-spec-list_ no longer fail (on those statements, at least).

This closes #5352 
This closes #3113 _implicit-spec-list_ in `IMPLICIT` statements","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Working with F77 validation suite FCVS_95 (see #480 ) demonstrated that our support for `IMPLICIT` statements was incomplete, particularly in that we only allow one _implicit-spec_ per _implicit-stmt_, instead of an _implicit-spec-list_.  An example statement that is now properly accepted is:
```f90
implicit real(i), integer(b)
```

 This PR adds that support, with changes in the AST grammar, the parser, AST symbol table visitor, and AST->src conversion.  AST->ASR conversion already supported this structure.

The PR also updates the test reference AST files, and adds the new test `implicit13`.  After these changes, FCVS_95 tests that use _implicit-spec-list_ no longer fail (on those statements, at least).

This closes #5352 
This closes #3113 statements","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Working with F77 validation suite FCVS_95 (see #480 ) demonstrated that our support for `IMPLICIT` statements was incomplete, particularly in that we only allow one _implicit-spec_ per _implicit-stmt_, instead of an _implicit-spec-list_.  An example statement that is now properly accepted is:
```f90
implicit real(i), integer(b)
```

 This PR adds that support, with changes in the AST grammar, the parser, AST symbol table visitor, and AST->src conversion.  AST->ASR conversion already supported this structure.

The PR also updates the test reference AST files, and adds the new test `implicit13`.  After these changes, FCVS_95 tests that use _implicit-spec-list_ no longer fail (on those statements, at least).

This closes #5352 
This closes #3113 statements","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Working with F77 validation suite FCVS_95 (see #480 ) demonstrated that our support for `IMPLICIT` statements was incomplete, particularly in that we only allow one _implicit-spec_ per _implicit-stmt_, instead of an _implicit-spec-list_.  An example statement that is now properly accepted is:
```f90
implicit real(i), integer(b)
```

 This PR adds that support, with changes in the AST grammar, the parser, AST symbol table visitor, and AST->src conversion.  AST->ASR conversion already supported this structure.

The PR also updates the test reference AST files, and adds the new test `implicit13`.  After these changes, FCVS_95 tests that use _implicit-spec-list_ no longer fail (on those statements, at least).

This closes #5352 
This closes #3113 statements","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Working with F77 validation suite FCVS_95 (see #480 ) demonstrated that our support for `IMPLICIT` statements was incomplete, particularly in that we only allow one _implicit-spec_ per _implicit-stmt_, instead of an _implicit-spec-list_.  An example statement that is now properly accepted is:
```f90
implicit real(i), integer(b)
```

 This PR adds that support, with changes in the AST grammar, the parser, AST symbol table visitor, and AST->src conversion.  AST->ASR conversion already supported this structure.

The PR also updates the test reference AST files, and adds the new test `implicit13`.  After these changes, FCVS_95 tests that use _implicit-spec-list_ no longer fail (on those statements, at least).

This closes #5352 
This closes #3113 statements","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Working with F77 validation suite FCVS_95 (see #480 ) demonstrated that our support for `IMPLICIT` statements was incomplete, particularly in that we only allow one _implicit-spec_ per _implicit-stmt_, instead of an _implicit-spec-list_.  An example statement that is now properly accepted is:
```f90
implicit real(i), integer(b)
```

 This PR adds that support, with changes in the AST grammar, the parser, AST symbol table visitor, and AST->src conversion.  AST->ASR conversion already supported this structure.

The PR also updates the test reference AST files, and adds the new test `implicit13`.  After these changes, FCVS_95 tests that use _implicit-spec-list_ no longer fail (on those statements, at least).

This closes #5352 
This closes #3113 statements","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Working with F77 validation suite FCVS_95 (see #480 ) demonstrated that our support for `IMPLICIT` statements was incomplete, particularly in that we only allow one _implicit-spec_ per _implicit-stmt_, instead of an _implicit-spec-list_.  An example statement that is now properly accepted is:
```f90
implicit real(i), integer(b)
```

 This PR adds that support, with changes in the AST grammar, the parser, AST symbol table visitor, and AST->src conversion.  AST->ASR conversion already supported this structure.

The PR also updates the test reference AST files, and adds the new test `implicit13`.  After these changes, FCVS_95 tests that use _implicit-spec-list_ no longer fail (on those statements, at least).

This closes #5352 
This closes #3113 _implicit-spec-list_ in `IMPLICIT` statements","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Working with F77 validation suite FCVS_95 (see #480 ) demonstrated that our support for `IMPLICIT` statements was incomplete, particularly in that we only allow one _implicit-spec_ per _implicit-stmt_, instead of an _implicit-spec-list_.  An example statement that is now properly accepted is:
```f90
implicit real(i), integer(b)
```

 This PR adds that support, with changes in the AST grammar, the parser, AST symbol table visitor, and AST->src conversion.  AST->ASR conversion already supported this structure.

The PR also updates the test reference AST files, and adds the new test `implicit13`.  After these changes, FCVS_95 tests that use _implicit-spec-list_ no longer fail (on those statements, at least).

{"action":"closed","number":5536,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/pulls/5536","id":2208615044,"node_id":"PR_kwDOCpMuHc6DpMqE","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/pull/5536","diff_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/pull/5536.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/pull/5536.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5536","number":5536,"state":"closed","locked":false,"title":"Support _implicit-spec-list_ in `IMPLICIT` statements","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Working with F77 validation suite FCVS_95 (see #480 ) demonstrated that our support for `IMPLICIT` statements was incomplete, particularly in that we only allow one _implicit-spec_ per _implicit-stmt_, instead of an _implicit-spec-list_.  An example statement that is now properly accepted is:
```f90
implicit real(i), integer(b)
```

 This PR adds that support, with changes in the AST grammar, the parser, AST symbol table visitor, and AST->src conversion.  AST->ASR conversion already supported this structure.

The PR also updates the test reference AST files, and adds the new test `implicit13`.  After these changes, FCVS_95 tests that use _implicit-spec-list_ no longer fail (on those statements, at least).

This closes #5352 
This closes #3113 {"action":"closed","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5352","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5352/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5352/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5352/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5352","id":2649953581,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6d8xUt","number":5352,"title":"IMPLICIT statement parsing error","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":4378061998,"node_id":"LA_kwDOCpMuHc8AAAABBPPsrg","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/labels/parser","name":"parser","color":"75B607","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-11-11T17:31:14Z","updated_at":"2024-12-01T21:41:06Z","closed_at":"2024-12-01T21:41:06Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Attempting to parse:
```f90
subroutine foo
  implicit integer(Y, V-X), real(M)
end subroutine foo
```
with LFortran SHA 4068df32e leads to the error:
```
syntax error: Token ',' is unexpected here
 --> func.f90:2:27
  |
2 |   implicit integer(Y, V-X), real(M)
  |                           ^ 
```
I'd guess that the grammar doesn't accept an _implicit-spec-list_, as is specified in the standard.

Also, _kind-selector_ inputs that aren't an `id` or `KW_INTEGER` are improperly rejected:
```f90
implicit real(4+4)(q-s)
implicit integer(kind=2)(i)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5352/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5352/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

{"repository_id":177417757,"push_id":21486907457,"size":6,"distinct_size":6,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"b55e6603cb8cb0e7111c76886367436d3ffede46","before":"c8ae673f811b4bdc89ee046d30bbab21cca7a286","commits":[{"sha":"deb230c3d52d1645776eae62e0fb2a71b66e3990","author":{"email":"df7d30f863ef98c5e68bada4edca4243f50fac4f@gmail.com","name":"Paul Henning"},"message":"Grammar and AST changes to support implicit-spec-list

Rather than `implicit_statement` directly accepting an _implicit-spec_,
break out the type and letter-spec-list into a new `implicit_spec`
rule, and accept a non-empty list of those for the IMPLICIT statement.
This is to allow standard Fortran such as:
```f90
implicit real(a), integer(b-o), complex(z)
```","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/commits/deb230c3d52d1645776eae62e0fb2a71b66e3990"},{"sha":"562ef51739ef3247b718e65dcfbf66e232223292","author":{"email":"df7d30f863ef98c5e68bada4edca4243f50fac4f@gmail.com","name":"Paul Henning"},"message":"Fix an output error in AST->src conversion of `IMPLICIT`","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/commits/562ef51739ef3247b718e65dcfbf66e232223292"},{"sha":"6199d6c79ac50abc8787034f1c81522d29c6ee79","author":{"email":"df7d30f863ef98c5e68bada4edca4243f50fac4f@gmail.com","name":"Paul Henning"},"message":"Update AST tests.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/commits/6199d6c79ac50abc8787034f1c81522d29c6ee79"},{"sha":"e349c97db70b59d5587d2930267de5143bbcca45","author":{"email":"df7d30f863ef98c5e68bada4edca4243f50fac4f@gmail.com","name":"Paul Henning"},"message":"Add a test for implicit-spec-list

working"},{"id":4378061998,"node_id":"LA_kwDOCpMuHc8AAAABBPPsrg","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/labels/parser","name":"parser","color":"75B607","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-01-13T22:05:49Z","updated_at":"2024-12-01T21:41:06Z","closed_at":"2024-12-01T21:41:06Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"MWE from the Netlib library dcdflib (https://netlib.org/random/):

```fortran
      DOUBLE PRECISION FUNCTION dstrem(z)
      IMPLICIT DOUBLE PRECISION (a-h,o-p,r-z),INTEGER (i-n),LOGICAL (q)
      dstrem = z
      END
```

```
$ lfortran -c --implicit-typing --fixed-form dcdflib_1.f 
syntax error: Token ',' is unexpected here
 --> dcdflib_1.f:2:46
  |
2 |       IMPLICIT DOUBLE PRECISION (a-h,o-p,r-z),INTEGER (i-n),LOGICAL (q)
  |                                              ^ 
```

A temporary workaround is to separate these into individual statements:
```fortran
      DOUBLE PRECISION FUNCTION dstrem(z)
      IMPLICIT DOUBLE PRECISION (a-h,o-p,r-z)
      IMPLICIT INTEGER (i-n)
      IMPLICIT LOGICAL (q)
      dstrem = z
      END
```

#4420 

We convert an `ArrayItem` like `x(loc, loc)`, where `loc` is an array, to:

```fortran
do __1_t = lbound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 1), ubound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 1)
    do __2_t = lbound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 2), ubound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 2)
        __libasr__created__var__0__array_item(__1_t, __2_t) = x(loc(__1_t), loc(__2_t))
    end do
end do
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549","id":2713710215,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6hv-6H","number":5549,"title":"IMPLICIT declaration_type_spec","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-12-03T00:51:25Z","updated_at":"2024-12-03T04:56:10Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently, the `parser.yy` grammar uses rules local to `implicit_spec` for imitating the _kind-selector_ component of an (intrinsic) _declaration-type-spec_ in an IMPLICIT statement.  While these rules handle some simple cases correctly (e.g. `implicit real(8) (a)`), it is easy to generate examples that should be accepted, but are not:
```f90
implicit real(kind=8) (a)
implicit real(4+4) (b)
implicit character(len=20, kind=c_char) (c)
```

Furthering the problem are inputs that are accepted using the `"(" letter_spec_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")"` style of rules, which are never checked or resolved as _scalar-int-constant-expr_:
```f90
program bad_kind
  use constants
  implicit real(r8k) (a)
  implicit real(dp) (b)
  implicit real(q-l) (c)
  print *, kind(a), kind(b), kind(c)
end program bad_kind
```
prints `4    4    4`, without even attempting to lookup values for `r8k` or `dp` or compute the value `q-l`.  Note that these problems are exhibited both before (SHA c75347178f1982e6) and after (SHA aed88c2ec20c7e4591) my commit to add _implicit-spec-list_ 

The appropriate solution to these problems is to change the grammar to simply use the `declaration_type_spec` rule, which handles all of these cases.  That would look like:
```bison
implicit_statement
    : KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE sep { $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE(TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE "(" implicit_none_spec_star ")" sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE2($4, TRIVIA_AFTER($6, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT($2, TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    ;

implicit_spec_list
    : implicit_spec_list "," implicit_spec { $$ = $1; LIST_ADD($$, $3); }
    | implicit_spec { LIST_NEW($$); LIST_ADD($$, $1); }
    ;

implicit_spec
    : declaration_type_spec "(" letter_spec_list ")" {
           $$ = IMPLICIT_SPEC($1, $3, @$); }
    ;
```
The complete implementation of this change can be seen in the draft PR linked below.

The reason that this is draft is that it appears to introduce five *essential* shift/reduce conflicts (see #5426), that don't go away in a standard LALR(1) grammar with reserved keywords.  I believe that this stems from the fact that the `letter_spec` rule, as implemented in LFortran, overlaps completely with the _kind-selector_ _scalar-int-constant-expr_:   letter-spec-lists like `(a)` and `(a-b)` can be parsed either as a letter-spec-list or as an expr. 

With the above grammar, Bison finds a shift/reduce conflict for the INTEGER, REAL, COMPLEX, and LOGICAL intrinsic types that take a _kind-selector_, and the CHARACTER type that takes a _char-selector_.  It eventually resolves it correctly by taking the shift option, but *I* don't know how to express the grammar in a way that prevents this ambiguity.

Using a newer version of Bison, we can get it to produce counterexamples that show the shift/reduce conflict.  This is the output for that diagnostic for a REAL declaration type spec:
```
State 480

  369 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL •  [TK_NAME, "/", ",", "(", ")", "::", KW_ABSTRACT, ..., KW_WHILE, KW_WRITE]
  370                    | KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
  371                    | KW_REAL • "*" TK_INTEGER

    "*"  shift, and go to state 505
    "("  shift, and go to state 506

    "("       [reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)]
    $default  reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)

    shift/reduce conflict on token "(":
      369 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL •
      370 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      First example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Shift derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                                            END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: implicit_statement
                  ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                                   sep
                                     ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                            ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec                         "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                   ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                          ↳ 370: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      Second example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Reduce derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                               END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: script_unit
                  ↳ 12: implicit_statement
                        ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                sep
                                           ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                                  ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec      "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                         ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                                ↳ 369: KW_REAL •
```
Details of this report can be found at: https://www.gnu.org/software/bison/manual/bison.html#Counterexamples

The PR referenced below compiles and works by extending the number of shift/reduce conflicts. #4420 

We convert an `ArrayItem` like `x(loc, loc)`, where `loc` is an array, to:

```fortran
do __1_t = lbound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 1), ubound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 1)
    do __2_t = lbound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 2), ubound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 2)
        __libasr__created__var__0__array_item(__1_t, __2_t) = x(loc(__1_t), loc(__2_t))
    end do
end do
```

#4420 

We convert an `ArrayItem` like `x(loc, loc)`, where `loc` is an array, to:

```fortran
do __1_t = lbound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 1), ubound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 1)
    do __2_t = lbound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 2), ubound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 2)
        __libasr__created__var__0__array_item(__1_t, __2_t) = x(loc(__1_t), loc(__2_t))
    end do
end do
```

#4420 

We convert an `ArrayItem` like `x(loc, loc)`, where `loc` is an array, to:

```fortran
do __1_t = lbound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 1), ubound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 1)
    do __2_t = lbound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 2), ubound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 2)
        __libasr__created__var__0__array_item(__1_t, __2_t) = x(loc(__1_t), loc(__2_t))
    end do
end do
```

### TODO:

program main
    integer, parameter :: RP = kind(0.0D0)
    integer, parameter :: IK = kind(0)
    real(RP) :: G(2, 2)
    integer(IK) :: knew
    real(RP):: zmat(1:5) 
    G = planerot(zmat([1, 2]))
    contains
    
    function planerot(x) result(G)
        real(RP), intent(in) :: x(:)
        real(RP) :: c, s, r
        real(RP) :: G(2, 2)
        c = x(1) / r
        G = reshape([c, -s, s, c], [2, 2])
    end function
end program

We have location information but it is incorrect.

```fortran
real function xyz()
        print *, "abc"
end function xyz

```


```console
% lfortran a.f90 --show-asr
Function: xyz
loc->first: 53763168

Function: xyz
loc->first: 53763168
loc->last: 24576
[...]

Function: xyz
loc->first: 53763168
loc->last: 24576
[...]

Function: xyz
loc->first: 53763168
loc->last: 24576
[...]

```fortran

end function xyz

```


```console

Function: xyz
loc->first: 53763168
loc->last: 24576
[...]

Function: xyz
loc->first: 53763168
loc->last: 24576
[...]

Function: xyz
loc->first: 53763168
loc->last: 24576
[...]

```fortran
module gg
    contains
    function func_arr3() result(ret_arr)
        integer,allocatable :: ret_arr(:)
        print *, allocated(ret_arr)
        allocate(ret_arr(1))
        ret_arr = [911]
        ! print *, "worked", inp_ref
    end function
end module


program p
    use gg
    implicit none
    integer, allocatable :: inp(:)
    integer :: i =77
    
    do i= 1, 3
        inp = func_arr3()
    end do 
end program

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549","id":2713710215,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6hv-6H","number":5549,"title":"IMPLICIT declaration_type_spec","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2024-12-03T00:51:25Z","updated_at":"2024-12-03T20:20:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently, the `parser.yy` grammar uses rules local to `implicit_spec` for imitating the _kind-selector_ component of an (intrinsic) _declaration-type-spec_ in an IMPLICIT statement.  While these rules handle some simple cases correctly (e.g. `implicit real(8) (a)`), it is easy to generate examples that should be accepted, but are not:
```f90
implicit real(kind=8) (a)
implicit real(4+4) (b)
implicit character(len=20, kind=c_char) (c)
```

Furthering the problem are inputs that are accepted using the `"(" letter_spec_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")"` style of rules, which are never checked or resolved as _scalar-int-constant-expr_:
```f90
program bad_kind
  use constants
  implicit real(r8k) (a)
  implicit real(dp) (b)
  implicit real(q-l) (c)
  print *, kind(a), kind(b), kind(c)
end program bad_kind
```
prints `4    4    4`, without even attempting to lookup values for `r8k` or `dp` or compute the value `q-l`.  Note that these problems are exhibited both before (SHA c75347178f1982e6) and after (SHA aed88c2ec20c7e4591) my commit to add _implicit-spec-list_ 

The appropriate solution to these problems is to change the grammar to simply use the `declaration_type_spec` rule, which handles all of these cases.  That would look like:
```bison
implicit_statement
    : KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE sep { $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE(TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE "(" implicit_none_spec_star ")" sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE2($4, TRIVIA_AFTER($6, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT($2, TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    ;

implicit_spec_list
    : implicit_spec_list "," implicit_spec { $$ = $1; LIST_ADD($$, $3); }
    | implicit_spec { LIST_NEW($$); LIST_ADD($$, $1); }
    ;

implicit_spec
    : declaration_type_spec "(" letter_spec_list ")" {
           $$ = IMPLICIT_SPEC($1, $3, @$); }
    ;
```
The complete implementation of this change can be seen in the draft PR linked below.

The reason that this is draft is that it appears to introduce five *essential* shift/reduce conflicts (see #5426), that don't go away in a standard LALR(1) grammar with reserved keywords.  I believe that this stems from the fact that the `letter_spec` rule, as implemented in LFortran, overlaps completely with the _kind-selector_ _scalar-int-constant-expr_:   letter-spec-lists like `(a)` and `(a-b)` can be parsed either as a letter-spec-list or as an expr. 

With the above grammar, Bison finds a shift/reduce conflict for the INTEGER, REAL, COMPLEX, and LOGICAL intrinsic types that take a _kind-selector_, and the CHARACTER type that takes a _char-selector_.  It eventually resolves it correctly by taking the shift option, but *I* don't know how to express the grammar in a way that prevents this ambiguity.

Using a newer version of Bison, we can get it to produce counterexamples that show the shift/reduce conflict.  This is the output for that diagnostic for a REAL declaration type spec:
```
State 480

  369 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL •  [TK_NAME, "/", ",", "(", ")", "::", KW_ABSTRACT, ..., KW_WHILE, KW_WRITE]
  370                    | KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
  371                    | KW_REAL • "*" TK_INTEGER

    "*"  shift, and go to state 505
    "("  shift, and go to state 506

    "("       [reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)]
    $default  reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)

    shift/reduce conflict on token "(":
      369 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL •
      370 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      First example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Shift derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                                            END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: implicit_statement
                  ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                                   sep
                                     ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                            ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec                         "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                   ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                          ↳ 370: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      Second example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Reduce derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                               END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: script_unit
                  ↳ 12: implicit_statement
                        ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                sep
                                           ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                                  ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec      "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                         ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                                ↳ 369: KW_REAL •
```
Details of this report can be found at: https://www.gnu.org/software/bison/manual/bison.html#Counterexamples

The PR referenced below compiles and works by extending the number of shift/reduce conflicts. guys, I really appreciate your amazing work that goes into LFortran. I thought, I'll test, how far I get with it to build the [Fortuno unit testing](https://github.com/fortuno-repos/fortuno) framework, to give further impulses for the development. :smile: 

I encountered a segfault when compiling the a given source file. The kind of minimal reproducer seems to be:
```fortran
module fortuno_basetypes
  implicit none

  type, abstract :: test_base
    character(:), allocatable :: name
  end type test_base


  type :: test_ptr_item
    class(test_base), pointer :: item => null()                                 
  end type test_ptr_item


  type :: test_list
    private
    type(test_ptr_item), pointer :: storage_(:) => null()                       
    integer :: nitems = 0
  contains
    procedure :: free => test_list_free
  end type


contains

  subroutine test_list_free(this)
    class(test_list), intent(inout) :: this                                     

    select type (item => this%storage_(1)%item)                                 
    class default
    end select

  end subroutine test_list_free

end module fortuno_basetypes
```

When running
```
❯ lfortran --tree -v -c basetypes.f90
```
it triggers 
```
zsh: segmentation fault (core dumped)  lfortran -v -c basetypes.f90
```
I am using the LFortran from conda-forge:
```
LFortran version: 0.42.0
Platform: Linux
LLVM: 19.1.1
Default target: x86_64-conda-linux-gnu
```
Please note, that the code snippet above is the minimal working example I could come up with and is not supposed to deliver something useable (but is standard conforming IMO). See the [complete source file](https://github.com/fortuno-repos/fortuno/blob/672a3981f6b0200d03ccce287c7722c5172e9636/src/fortuno/basetypes.f90) for further details.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5515/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5515/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2515489201","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5515#issuecomment-2515489201","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5515","id":2515489201,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6V71Gx","user":{"login":"certik","id":20568,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIwNTY4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/20568?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik","html_url":"https://github.com/certik","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-03T20:29:30Z","updated_at":"2024-12-03T20:29:30Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"@aradi thanks for the bug report. Let's just call it `--linker`. There is another problem --- each linker has different command line handling, so LFortran needs to know the type. On Windows use use the MSVC `link`.

I recommend the following:
* `--linker=clang/gcc/msvc/lld` specifies the type of the linker, so that LFortran knows.
* `--linker-path` can provide optional full path to the linker executable, otherwise just the one from path is used
* We do not check if it is available (wastes time), we simply call it with the options we understand about the linker (for example for `msvc` we use the `msvc` options, and if the user supplied clang instead, it will fail). We check the exit code, if it failed, we exit. That's it.
* The default should be `msvc` on Windows, `clang` on macOS and freeBSD and either `clang` or `gcc` on Linux (not sure which compiler is the more common default system compiler, likely `gcc`, so let's use `gcc` on Linux).

By default things should just work on all platforms with no extra options. Sometimes the user might use `--linker` to select a different linker (say `clang` on Linux from the default `gcc`).

Regarding `LFORTRAN_CC`, we should also support it, but we need to somehow tell LFortran what linker it is. So how about `LFORTRAN_LINKER` and `LFORTRAN_LINKER_PATH` which would be equivalent to `--linker` and `--linker-path`?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2515693269/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

While these rules handle some simple cases correctly (e.g. `implicit real(8) (a)`), it is easy to generate examples that should be accepted, but are not:
```f90
implicit real(kind=8) (a)
implicit real(4+4) (b)
implicit character(len=20, kind=c_char) (c)
```

Furthering the problem are inputs that are accepted using the `"(" letter_spec_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")"` style of rules, which are never checked or resolved as _scalar-int-constant-expr_:
```f90
program bad_kind
  use constants
  implicit real(r8k) (a)
  implicit real(dp) (b)
  implicit real(q-l) (c)
  print *, kind(a), kind(b), kind(c)
end program bad_kind
```
prints `4    4    4`, without even attempting to lookup values for `r8k` or `dp` or compute the value `q-l`.  Note that these problems are exhibited both before (SHA c75347178f1982e6) and after (SHA aed88c2ec20c7e4591) my commit to add _implicit-spec-list_ 

The appropriate solution to these problems is to change the grammar to simply use the `declaration_type_spec` rule, which handles all of these cases.  That would look like:
```bison
implicit_statement
    : KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE sep { $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE(TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE "(" implicit_none_spec_star ")" sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE2($4, TRIVIA_AFTER($6, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT($2, TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    ;

implicit_spec_list
    : implicit_spec_list "," implicit_spec { $$ = $1; LIST_ADD($$, $3); }
    | implicit_spec { LIST_NEW($$); LIST_ADD($$, $1); }
    ;

implicit_spec
    : declaration_type_spec "(" letter_spec_list ")" {
           $$ = IMPLICIT_SPEC($1, $3, @$); }
    ;
```
The complete implementation of this change can be seen in the draft PR linked below.

The reason that this is draft is that it appears to introduce five *essential* shift/reduce conflicts (see #5426), that don't go away in a standard LALR(1) grammar with reserved keywords.  I believe that this stems from the fact that the `letter_spec` rule, as implemented in LFortran, overlaps completely with the _kind-selector_ _scalar-int-constant-expr_:   letter-spec-lists like `(a)` and `(a-b)` can be parsed either as a letter-spec-list or as an expr. 

With the above grammar, Bison finds a shift/reduce conflict for the INTEGER, REAL, COMPLEX, and LOGICAL intrinsic types that take a _kind-selector_, and the CHARACTER type that takes a _char-selector_.  It eventually resolves it correctly by taking the shift option, but *I* don't know how to express the grammar in a way that prevents this ambiguity.

Using a newer version of Bison, we can get it to produce counterexamples that show the shift/reduce conflict.  This is the output for that diagnostic for a REAL declaration type spec:
```
State 480

  369 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL •  [TK_NAME, "/", ",", "(", ")", "::", KW_ABSTRACT, ..., KW_WHILE, KW_WRITE]
  370                    | KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
  371                    | KW_REAL • "*" TK_INTEGER

    "*"  shift, and go to state 505
    "("  shift, and go to state 506

    "("       [reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)]
    $default  reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)

    shift/reduce conflict on token "(":
      369 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL •
      370 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      First example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Shift derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                                            END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: implicit_statement
                  ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                                   sep
                                     ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                            ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec                         "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                   ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                          ↳ 370: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      Second example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Reduce derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                               END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: script_unit
                  ↳ 12: implicit_statement
                        ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                sep
                                           ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                                  ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec      "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                         ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                                ↳ 369: KW_REAL •
```
Details of this report can be found at: https://www.gnu.org/software/bison/manual/bison.html#Counterexamples

The PR referenced below compiles and works by extending the number of shift/reduce conflicts.  I'm not sure how to proceed: all suggestions are welcome!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2515705855","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549#issuecomment-2515705855","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549","id":2515705855,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6V8p__","user":{"login":"certik","id":20568,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIwNTY4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/20568?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik","html_url":"https://github.com/certik","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-03T22:46:05Z","updated_at":"2024-12-03T22:46:05Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Note that in the PR I changed it to:
```
implicit_spec
    : KW_INTEGER "(" kind_arg_list ")" %dprec 3 {
           $$ = IMPLICIT_SPEC(ATTR_TYPE(Integer, @$), $3, @$); }
    | KW_INTEGER "(" kind_arg_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")" %dprec 4 {
           $$ = IMPLICIT_SPEC(ATTR_TYPE_KIND(Integer, $3, @$), $6, @$); }
    ;
```
The first one has to be `kind_arg_list`, otherwise you indeed get reduce/reduce conflicts, because the letter "a" in `letter_spec_list` can also be an expression in `kind_arg_list`. So when you write `implicit real (a)` or `implicit real (a-f)`, it is actually parsed as `kind_arg_list`, where "a-f" is an "expr". Then in AST->ASR we know that in this case we need to extract it. We could actually do that directly in the `IMPLICIT_SPEC` macro also, I think this is a simple transformation.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2515705855/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549","id":2713710215,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6hv-6H","number":5549,"title":"IMPLICIT declaration_type_spec","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":13,"created_at":"2024-12-03T00:51:25Z","updated_at":"2024-12-03T23:47:43Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently, the `parser.yy` grammar uses rules local to `implicit_spec` for imitating the _kind-selector_ component of an (intrinsic) _declaration-type-spec_ in an IMPLICIT statement.  While these rules handle some simple cases correctly (e.g. `implicit real(8) (a)`), it is easy to generate examples that should be accepted, but are not:
```f90
implicit real(kind=8) (a)
implicit real(4+4) (b)
implicit character(len=20, kind=c_char) (c)
```

Furthering the problem are inputs that are accepted using the `"(" letter_spec_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")"` style of rules, which are never checked or resolved as _scalar-int-constant-expr_:
```f90
program bad_kind
  use constants
  implicit real(r8k) (a)
  implicit real(dp) (b)
  implicit real(q-l) (c)
  print *, kind(a), kind(b), kind(c)
end program bad_kind
```
prints `4    4    4`, without even attempting to lookup values for `r8k` or `dp` or compute the value `q-l`.  Note that these problems are exhibited both before (SHA c75347178f1982e6) and after (SHA aed88c2ec20c7e4591) my commit to add _implicit-spec-list_ 

The appropriate solution to these problems is to change the grammar to simply use the `declaration_type_spec` rule, which handles all of these cases.  That would look like:
```bison
implicit_statement
    : KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE sep { $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE(TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE "(" implicit_none_spec_star ")" sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE2($4, TRIVIA_AFTER($6, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT($2, TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    ;

implicit_spec_list
    : implicit_spec_list "," implicit_spec { $$ = $1; LIST_ADD($$, $3); }
    | implicit_spec { LIST_NEW($$); LIST_ADD($$, $1); }
    ;

implicit_spec
    : declaration_type_spec "(" letter_spec_list ")" {
           $$ = IMPLICIT_SPEC($1, $3, @$); }
    ;
```
The complete implementation of this change can be seen in the draft PR linked below.

The reason that this is draft is that it appears to introduce five *essential* shift/reduce conflicts (see #5426), that don't go away in a standard LALR(1) grammar with reserved keywords.  I believe that this stems from the fact that the `letter_spec` rule, as implemented in LFortran, overlaps completely with the _kind-selector_ _scalar-int-constant-expr_:   letter-spec-lists like `(a)` and `(a-b)` can be parsed either as a letter-spec-list or as an expr. 

With the above grammar, Bison finds a shift/reduce conflict for the INTEGER, REAL, COMPLEX, and LOGICAL intrinsic types that take a _kind-selector_, and the CHARACTER type that takes a _char-selector_.  It eventually resolves it correctly by taking the shift option, but *I* don't know how to express the grammar in a way that prevents this ambiguity.

Using a newer version of Bison, we can get it to produce counterexamples that show the shift/reduce conflict.  This is the output for that diagnostic for a REAL declaration type spec:
```
State 480

  369 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL •  [TK_NAME, "/", ",", "(", ")", "::", KW_ABSTRACT, ..., KW_WHILE, KW_WRITE]
  370                    | KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
  371                    | KW_REAL • "*" TK_INTEGER

    "*"  shift, and go to state 505
    "("  shift, and go to state 506

    "("       [reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)]
    $default  reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)

    shift/reduce conflict on token "(":
      369 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL •
      370 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      First example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Shift derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                                            END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: implicit_statement
                  ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                                   sep
                                     ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                            ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec                         "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                   ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                          ↳ 370: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      Second example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Reduce derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                               END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: script_unit
                  ↳ 12: implicit_statement
                        ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                sep
                                           ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                                  ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec      "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                         ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                                ↳ 369: KW_REAL •
```
Details of this report can be found at: https://www.gnu.org/software/bison/manual/bison.html#Counterexamples

The PR referenced below compiles and works by extending the number of shift/reduce conflicts. We have location information which seems correct to me

```fortran
module module_function_call1
    type :: softmax
    contains
      procedure :: eval_1d
    end type softmax
  contains
  
    pure function eval_1d(self, x) result(res)
      class(softmax), intent(in) :: self
      real, intent(in) :: x(:)
      real :: res(size(x))
    end function eval_1d
  
    pure function eval_1d_prime(self, x) result(res)
      class(softmax), intent(in) :: self
      real, intent(in) :: x(:)
      real :: res(size(x))
      res = self%eval_1d(x)
    end function eval_1d_prime
end module module_function_call1
```

```

```fortran
program file_read
implicit none
integer :: rows, cols, i
character(len=:), allocatable :: lines(:)
integer :: u
open(newunit=u, file="input.txt", status='old')
rows = 2
cols = 2
print *, rows, cols
allocate(character(len=cols) :: lines(rows))
do i = 1, rows
    print *, i
    read(u, '(A)') lines(i) ! fails here
    print *, lines(i)
end do
close(u)
end program
```
Here is the `input.txt`:
```
xx
yy
```
In GFortran:
```console
$ gfortran a.f90 && ./a.out 
           2           2
           1
 xx
           2
 yy
```
And LFortran:
```console
$ lfortran a.f90 
2    2
1
```

The following diff makes it work:
```diff
--- a/a.f90
+++ b/a.f90
@@ -2,6 +2,7 @@ program file_read
 implicit none
 integer :: rows, cols, i
 character(len=:), allocatable :: lines(:)
+character(len=100) :: tmp
 integer :: u
 open(newunit=u, file="input.txt", status='old')
 rows = 2
@@ -10,7 +11,8 @@ print *, rows, cols
 allocate(character(len=cols) :: lines(rows))
 do i = 1, rows
     print *, i
-    read(u, '(A)') lines(i) ! fails here
+    read(u, '(A)') tmp ! fails here
+    lines(i) = tmp
     print *, lines(i)
 end do
 close(u)
```
Then:
```console
$ gfortran a.f90 && ./a.out
           2           2
           1
 xx
           2
 yy
$ lfortran a.f90           
2    2
1
xx
2
yy
```

So the bug is just reading into an allocatable array of strings. Reading into just one string and then assigning to the allocatable array works.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5575/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5575/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

code"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-11-12T07:32:21Z","updated_at":"2024-12-04T17:58:02Z","closed_at":"2024-12-04T17:58:02Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"```fortran 
module inf_mod_1
interface is_finite
    module procedure is_finite_1
end interface is_finite
contains
subroutine is_finite_1() 
end subroutine is_finite_1
end module inf_mod_1

module infnan_mod_1
use, non_intrinsic :: inf_mod_1, only : is_finite
contains
subroutine is_finite_1(x)
integer , intent(in) :: x
print *, x 
end subroutine is_finite_1
end module infnan_mod_1


program bobyqa_exmp
use, non_intrinsic :: infnan_mod_1
call is_finite_1(1)
end program bobyqa_exmp
```

LFortran Error :
```console
semantic error: More actual than formal arguments in procedure call
  --> integration_tests/a.f90:22:18
   |
22 | call is_finite_1(1)
   |                  ^ 
```

Here issue is in SymbolTable of program `bobyqa_exmp` where `is_finte_1` is stored as ExternalSymbol for is_finite_1 of inf_mod_1 and we have no information of is_finite_1 of infnan_mod_1. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549","id":2713710215,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6hv-6H","number":5549,"title":"IMPLICIT declaration_type_spec","user":{"login":"pjh40","id":40867327,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY3MzI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40867327?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40","html_url":"https://github.com/pjh40","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pjh40/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":15,"created_at":"2024-12-03T00:51:25Z","updated_at":"2024-12-04T18:14:08Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently, the `parser.yy` grammar uses rules local to `implicit_spec` for imitating the _kind-selector_ component of an (intrinsic) _declaration-type-spec_ in an IMPLICIT statement.  While these rules handle some simple cases correctly (e.g. `implicit real(8) (a)`), it is easy to generate examples that should be accepted, but are not:
```f90
implicit real(kind=8) (a)
implicit real(4+4) (b)
implicit character(len=20, kind=c_char) (c)
```

Furthering the problem are inputs that are accepted using the `"(" letter_spec_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")"` style of rules, which are never checked or resolved as _scalar-int-constant-expr_:
```f90
program bad_kind
  use constants
  implicit real(r8k) (a)
  implicit real(dp) (b)
  implicit real(q-l) (c)
  print *, kind(a), kind(b), kind(c)
end program bad_kind
```
prints `4    4    4`, without even attempting to lookup values for `r8k` or `dp` or compute the value `q-l`.  Note that these problems are exhibited both before (SHA c75347178f1982e6) and after (SHA aed88c2ec20c7e4591) my commit to add _implicit-spec-list_ 

The appropriate solution to these problems is to change the grammar to simply use the `declaration_type_spec` rule, which handles all of these cases.  That would look like:
```bison
implicit_statement
    : KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE sep { $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE(TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT KW_NONE "(" implicit_none_spec_star ")" sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT_NONE2($4, TRIVIA_AFTER($6, @$), @$); }
    | KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list sep {
            $$ = IMPLICIT($2, TRIVIA_AFTER($3, @$), @$); }
    ;

implicit_spec_list
    : implicit_spec_list "," implicit_spec { $$ = $1; LIST_ADD($$, $3); }
    | implicit_spec { LIST_NEW($$); LIST_ADD($$, $1); }
    ;

implicit_spec
    : declaration_type_spec "(" letter_spec_list ")" {
           $$ = IMPLICIT_SPEC($1, $3, @$); }
    ;
```
The complete implementation of this change can be seen in the draft PR linked below.

The reason that this is draft is that it appears to introduce five *essential* shift/reduce conflicts (see #5426), that don't go away in a standard LALR(1) grammar with reserved keywords.  I believe that this stems from the fact that the `letter_spec` rule, as implemented in LFortran, overlaps completely with the _kind-selector_ _scalar-int-constant-expr_:   letter-spec-lists like `(a)` and `(a-b)` can be parsed either as a letter-spec-list or as an expr. 

With the above grammar, Bison finds a shift/reduce conflict for the INTEGER, REAL, COMPLEX, and LOGICAL intrinsic types that take a _kind-selector_, and the CHARACTER type that takes a _char-selector_.  It eventually resolves it correctly by taking the shift option, but *I* don't know how to express the grammar in a way that prevents this ambiguity.

Using a newer version of Bison, we can get it to produce counterexamples that show the shift/reduce conflict.  This is the output for that diagnostic for a REAL declaration type spec:
```
State 480

  369 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL •  [TK_NAME, "/", ",", "(", ")", "::", KW_ABSTRACT, ..., KW_WHILE, KW_WRITE]
  370                    | KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
  371                    | KW_REAL • "*" TK_INTEGER

    "*"  shift, and go to state 505
    "("  shift, and go to state 506

    "("       [reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)]
    $default  reduce using rule 369 (intrinsic_type_spec)

    shift/reduce conflict on token "(":
      369 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL •
      370 intrinsic_type_spec: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      First example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")" "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Shift derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                                            END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: implicit_statement
                  ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                                   sep
                                     ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                            ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec                         "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                   ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                          ↳ 370: KW_REAL • "(" kind_arg_list ")"
      Second example: KW_IMPLICIT KW_REAL • "(" letter_spec_list ")" sep END_OF_FILE
      Reduce derivation
        $accept
        ↳ 0: units                                                                                               END_OF_FILE
             ↳ 2: script_unit
                  ↳ 12: implicit_statement
                        ↳ 221: KW_IMPLICIT implicit_spec_list                                                sep
                                           ↳ 223: implicit_spec
                                                  ↳ 224: declaration_type_spec      "(" letter_spec_list ")"
                                                         ↳ 382: intrinsic_type_spec
                                                                ↳ 369: KW_REAL •
```
Details of this report can be found at: https://www.gnu.org/software/bison/manual/bison.html#Counterexamples

The PR referenced below compiles and works by extending the number of shift/reduce conflicts.  I'm not sure how to proceed: all suggestions are welcome!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2518190741","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549#issuecomment-2518190741","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5549","id":2518190741,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6WGIqV","user":{"login":"certik","id":20568,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIwNTY4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/20568?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik","html_url":"https://github.com/certik","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/certik/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-04T18:14:06Z","updated_at":"2024-12-04T18:14:06Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"The product type `(...)` you choose if there is just one option/line in the sum type.
